news_summary: Recent advancements in AI models demonstrate China's growing competitiveness with US counterparts, while new scientific approaches emphasize future-proofing research through improved foresight.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès récents en matière de modèles d'intelligence artificielle témoignent de la compétitivité croissante de la Chine face à ses homologues américains, tandis que de nouvelles approches scientifiques mettent l'accent sur une recherche tournée vers l'avenir grâce à une meilleure prospective.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances en los modelos de inteligencia artificial ponen de manifiesto la creciente competitividad de China frente a sus homólogos estadounidenses, mientras que los nuevos enfoques científicos hacen hincapié en orientar la investigación hacia el futuro mediante una mejor previsión.

articles.title: Does China’s latest AI model finally equal US rivals? What scientists think
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 21 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02281-2‘Kimi K3’ can match or outperform cutting-edge models, says Moonshot AI. But its large size might limit uptake.
articles.content:
Credit: CN-STR/AFP via Getty

The latest Chinese-built large language model (LLM) is impressing scientists with its size and capabilities. Last week, Beijing-based Moonshot AI unveiled ‘Kimi K3’, a powerful reasoning LLM that can handle large swathes of text. The company’s own tests found that K3 can match or outperform rival US models on tasks such as coding and manipulating spreadsheets.

Not since DeepSeek has a Chinese artificial-intelligence model caused quite so much buzz. “It is a turning point,” says Joel Pearson, a cognitive neuroscientist at UNSW Sydney in Australia, who studies how AI is affecting people’s lives. “People are calling it a ‘Sputnik moment’,” he adds.

Three days after the model’s release, Moonshot AI said it had paused new sign-ups to K3 because demand had pushed its system close to the limits of its processing capacity.

The model’s launch, on 16 July, came just before the 2026 Artificial Intelligence World Conference in Shanghai. The conference began with Chinese President Xi Jinping announcing the formal start of a global alliance to create regulation that ensures AI is safe and benefits people. “In China’s view, all countries should take a people-centred approach and develop AI for the positive and for good,” Xi said at the conference, adding that AI should be a driver for “shared prosperity and common security”.

The timing of Kimi K3’s release is significant, says Mehwish Nasim, an AI researcher at the University of Western Australia in Perth. The model was launched just weeks after Claude Fable 5 by US company Anthropic, and days after OpenAI released its latest GPT-5.6 model. “China is signalling its ambition not only to build frontier AI systems but also to help shape the international AI ecosystem and its governance,” she says.

Largest open-weight model

K3 is open weight, like its predecessor Kimi K2 and DeepSeek models, which were developed by a Hangzhou-based firm of the same name, meaning its core components are publicly available and can be downloaded and modified by researchers for free, while information on the model’s training is private. It can also be accessed using an ‘application programming interface’, which allows software to communicate, at a lower cost than proprietary LLMs, such as those owned by OpenAI, Google and Anthropic. Those LLMs are closed weight, meaning they cannot be downloaded and modified.

Moonshot says that the weights, or parameters, will be released on 27 July. Rahul Shome, a robotics and AI researcher at the Australian National University in Canberra, says that there has been a push from consumers, developers and researchers to make AI models open weight. K3 is particularly exciting because it would be the largest open-weight model, says Shome, with 2.8 trillion parameters and a working memory of one million tokens (the units of text used by AI models). The model is too large to run on personal devices, however, so it will probably require significant investment by institutions to run it, he adds.

K3’s large working memory means it could remember the contents of thousands of lines of code or a whole book and reduce the likelihood of producing fabricated information called hallucination, says Niusha Shafiabady, a computational-intelligence researcher at Australian Catholic University in Sydney. Shafiabady says that she is going to test K3 for herself by asking it to summarize research findings.

Closing the gap

The performance and capabilities of open-weight models have historically lagged behind those of US proprietary ones. But the gap seems to be the tightest it has ever been, says Aaron Snoswell, an AI accountability researcher at the Queensland University of Technology Generative AI Lab, Brisbane.

Pearson says that Chinese open-weight models are changing how investors and users perceive the value of US frontier models. Google, Anthropic and OpenAI spend billions of dollars training their models compared with the much lower budgets of Chinese companies. Moreover, users could lose access to US models if the government decides to impose restrictions, says Pearson. Last month, Anthropic temporarily disabled its most advanced models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, for all users after it was directed by the US government to suspend access for foreign nationals. Access to Fable 5 was restored this month, but Mythos 5 is only available to specific US organizations.

Toby Walsh, a computer scientist at UNSW, says that Chinese AI companies have been able to build frontier AI models despite the United States restricting China’s access to advanced AI chips, citing national-security concerns. “One suspects that necessity here was the mother of invention,” he adds.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough with the launch of China's large open-weight AI model Kimi K3, which matches or outperforms leading US models. This development has broad implications for global AI innovation, accessibility, and governance, fostering competition and collaboration in AI technology with potential widespread societal benefits.
articles.personality_title: China’s new AI model Kimi K3 matches top US systems in key tests
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – China has been developing large language models (LLMs), which are advanced computer programs that can understand and generate human-like text. Until now, US companies have led this technology with closed models that only they can control.

**What happened** – On July 16, 2026, Moonshot AI, a company based in Beijing, released Kimi K3, a new large language model. Tests showed that Kimi K3 can perform as well as or better than top US models in tasks like coding and working with spreadsheets. Unlike many US models, Kimi K3 is open weight, meaning researchers can download and modify it freely. It has 2.8 trillion parameters and can handle very long texts, like entire books.

**Impact** – Kimi K3 is the largest open-weight AI model so far, which is important because it gives more people access to powerful AI tools. Its ability to remember large amounts of information helps reduce errors often seen in AI, like making up facts. This breakthrough narrows the gap between Chinese and US AI technology, showing China’s growing strength in this field despite limited access to advanced computer chips.

**What's next step** – Moonshot AI plans to release the full model parameters to the public on July 27, allowing more researchers to explore and improve it. This move may encourage more open collaboration worldwide. Meanwhile, the large size of Kimi K3 means it will mostly be used by big institutions rather than on personal devices, at least for now.

**One-sentence takeaway** – China’s Kimi K3 is a powerful and openly accessible AI model that rivals top US systems, marking a new phase in global AI development.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le nouveau modèle d’IA chinois Kimi K3 rivalise avec les meilleurs systèmes américains
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Chine développe des grands modèles de langage (LLM), des programmes informatiques capables de comprendre et de générer du texte humain. Jusqu’à présent, les entreprises américaines dominaient cette technologie avec des modèles fermés qu’elles contrôlent seules.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 16 juillet 2026, Moonshot AI, une entreprise basée à Pékin, a lancé Kimi K3, un nouveau grand modèle de langage. Des tests ont montré que Kimi K3 peut égaler ou dépasser les meilleurs modèles américains dans des tâches comme la programmation et la gestion de tableaux. Contrairement à beaucoup de modèles américains, Kimi K3 est « open weight », ce qui signifie que les chercheurs peuvent le télécharger et le modifier librement. Il possède 2,8 billions de paramètres et peut gérer des textes très longs, comme des livres entiers.

**Impact** – Kimi K3 est le plus grand modèle d’IA open weight à ce jour, ce qui est important car il donne accès à des outils puissants à plus de personnes. Sa capacité à mémoriser beaucoup d’informations aide à réduire les erreurs fréquentes comme la fabrication de faits. Cette avancée réduit l’écart entre la Chine et les États-Unis en IA, montrant la montée en puissance de la Chine malgré un accès limité aux puces informatiques avancées.

**Prochaine étape** – Moonshot AI prévoit de rendre publics les paramètres complets du modèle le 27 juillet, permettant à plus de chercheurs de l’explorer et de l’améliorer. Ce geste pourrait encourager une collaboration plus ouverte dans le monde entier. Cependant, la grande taille de Kimi K3 signifie qu’il sera surtout utilisé par de grandes institutions plutôt que sur des appareils personnels, pour l’instant.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Kimi K3, modèle d’IA puissant et accessible, rivalise avec les meilleurs systèmes américains, marquant une nouvelle étape dans le développement mondial de l’IA.
articles.personality_title_es: El nuevo modelo de IA chino Kimi K3 iguala a los mejores sistemas estadounidenses
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – China ha estado desarrollando grandes modelos de lenguaje (LLM), programas informáticos avanzados que pueden entender y generar texto similar al humano. Hasta ahora, las empresas estadounidenses han liderado esta tecnología con modelos cerrados que solo ellas controlan.

**Qué pasó** – El 16 de julio de 2026, Moonshot AI, una compañía con sede en Pekín, lanzó Kimi K3, un nuevo gran modelo de lenguaje. Las pruebas mostraron que Kimi K3 puede igualar o superar a los mejores modelos estadounidenses en tareas como programación y manejo de hojas de cálculo. A diferencia de muchos modelos estadounidenses, Kimi K3 es de código abierto, lo que permite a los investigadores descargarlo y modificarlo libremente. Tiene 2.8 billones de parámetros y puede manejar textos muy largos, como libros enteros.

**Impacto** – Kimi K3 es el modelo de IA de código abierto más grande hasta ahora, lo que es importante porque da acceso a herramientas potentes a más personas. Su capacidad para recordar mucha información ayuda a reducir errores comunes en la IA, como inventar datos. Este avance reduce la brecha entre la tecnología de IA china y estadounidense, mostrando la creciente fuerza de China a pesar del acceso limitado a chips avanzados.

**Próximo paso** – Moonshot AI planea publicar los parámetros completos del modelo el 27 de julio, permitiendo que más investigadores lo exploren y mejoren. Este movimiento podría fomentar una colaboración más abierta a nivel mundial. Sin embargo, el gran tamaño de Kimi K3 significa que probablemente será usado principalmente por grandes instituciones y no en dispositivos personales, al menos por ahora.

**Frase clave** – Kimi K3 es un modelo de IA potente y de acceso abierto que compite con los mejores sistemas estadounidenses, marcando una nueva fase en el desarrollo global de la IA.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of two intricately designed, glowing neural network structures—one styled with subtle red and gold hues symbolizing China, the other with blue and silver tones representing the US—intertwined and connected by streams of flowing data threads over a softly lit abstract map backdrop, conveying collaboration and competition in advanced open-weight AI models.

articles.title: The science of foresight: how to future-proof your research
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 20 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02231-yAnticipating from the start the various ways a research programme can succeed or fail can avoid problems later. Here’s how to do it.
articles.content:
Every research programme is a bet on the future. Yet, scientists rarely examine the assumptions behind those bets. Decisions about grants, infrastructure, hiring, regulation and training often presume that certain technologies will mature, specific skills will be needed, the public will accept the resulting innovations and few risks will materialize. Yet these assumptions are rarely stated, let alone tested or revised in a systematic way1.

The consequence is a mismatch between current research activity and future conditions, a gap that will only grow with the pace of discovery and global change. If they don’t think ahead, scientific institutions will increasingly find themselves pressed to swiftly revise research priorities, training plans and infrastructure investments after a crisis emerges for which they were unprepared.

Could agentic AI topple grant-funding systems?

During the COVID-19 pandemic, for example, researchers had to accelerate vaccine development, scale up telemedicine, redeploy clinical staff, move education online and manage public trust in surveillance and vaccination. They had to do this under crisis conditions and with little initial knowledge of the virus2.

The rapid rise of artificial intelligence is also hard to navigate. Institutions must make decisions about validation, evaluation methods, workforce preparedness, research integrity and governance before long-term evidence is available3.

What is missing is not perfect prediction, but foresight: a structured, forward-looking process for examining multiple plausible futures, identifying the assumptions that matter across them, and defining signals that can trigger changes in research direction, staffing and funding priorities.

The field of ‘futures studies’ offers rigorous methods for analysing trends, planning for a range of outcomes and using ‘horizon scanning’ to scope out future risks and opportunities. Yet these methods aren’t mainstream in scientific practice. It’s time for that to change.

Here, we call for futures methods to be plugged into the core mechanisms of scientific development, to inform decisions about what science should fund, build, teach, validate and evaluate. We call this concept translational foresight. Adopting it would make the promises embedded in research programmes explicit, testable, traceable and revisable (see ‘Translational foresight’).

Looking ahead

Translational foresight has parallels with translational medicine. In the late twentieth century, molecular biology was advancing rapidly, but that knowledge was not reaching the clinic4. Expressions in medical research such as “bench to bedside” and “crossing the valley of death” highlighted this gap5. In response, scientists reoriented their systems to link discovery to patient outcomes6. This was accompanied by the rise of clinician-scientist training programmes7, changes in the funding model for translational research and the development of dedicated institutions for translational medicine8.

Similarly, the futures-studies field has established methods for exploring future trends, uncertainties and alternative trajectories. These methods are not routinely used in science-research contexts.

The Futures Wheel is a method for graphically visualizing direct and indirect consequences (see ‘Futures-thinking tools’). It was used by some governments to map cascading impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic, revealing shifts towards telemedicine, workforce strain and changes in care delivery, for instance9.

Futures-thinking tools Exercises to explore future research directions and anticipate problems. Method Purpose How to Run It (60–90-minute version) Output When to Use The Futures Wheel Explore consequences of a trend or milestone State a central change, such as “AI can now co-author scientific papers”; list direct and indirect effects; highlight unintended consequences. Map of risks, opportunities and ripple effects Before adopting a technology, submitting a grant or implementing a policy 2×2 Scenario Analysis (One Driver, One Uncertainty) Structure uncertainty into four plausible futures Choose one key driver, such as AI growth, and one uncertainty, such as regulation; combine them into four scenarios; ask which strategies work across all four. Four distinct future worlds; list of robust versus fragile strategies Strategic planning, long-term research direction, infrastructure investment Vision Writing (Backcasting Narrative) Define the desired long-term impact Choose a time horizon, such as 2035; write from the perspective of a future researcher or clinician; identify what changed, what worked and what failed. Clarify long-term aspirations and milestones Setting a laboratory mission, forming an institution Stump the Futurist (Research Version) Stress-test assumptions and blind spots Propose a future claim; challenge it with failure modes and missing variables; revise the claim with explicit conditions. Refined assumptions and conditional forecasts Grant preparation, technology assessment, proposing controversial hypotheses

Scenario analysis is another technique that has informed long-term strategy in industry and policy. For example, energy company Shell has utilized it to respond to energy crises, and governments have used it to develop national policies on climate, infrastructure and mobility.

Forecasting platforms, such as the Good Judgment Project, which is led by researchers at the University of Pennsylvania in Philadelphia, harness the wisdom of the crowd to answer forward-looking questions across the political, economic and social spectrum.

Without such tools, science relies on informal judgment, intuition and short-term planning to anticipate its future implications.

Science in 2050: the future breakthroughs that will shape our world — and beyond

Pandemic-preparedness frameworks, for example, can identify biological and logistical risks, such as whether health systems have enough tests, vaccines, beds, protective equipment and emergency plans. But they rarely shape research priorities, workforce planning or technology validation needs before a crisis begins10.

Translational foresight turns preparedness from a static emergency checklist into a system for testing and updating the assumptions on which future decisions depend. For example, funders could support the testing of remote care, home monitoring and rapid-diagnostics systems before they are urgently needed. Health systems could define trigger points in advance — from occupancy of intensive-care units to levels of public distrust — that would prompt revision of research, staffing or funding targets.

Similarly, personalized and genomics-based vaccine research involves not only designing vaccines, but also making assumptions about the future world in which they will be used11. Scientists and funders implicitly assume who the target populations will be, how microbes will evolve, what genomic data will be available, whether regulators will accept personalized vaccine strategies and whether the public will trust the vaccines. Recognizing these assumptions at the start will enable projects that can adapt if conditions change.

The great university shake-up: four charts show how global higher education is changing

The challenge lies in translating the outputs of futures methodologies into research priorities, funding decisions, infrastructure planning and evaluation. Several barriers have kept this translation from becoming routine.
articles.reasoning: The article promotes the adoption of structured foresight methods in scientific research to better anticipate future challenges and opportunities, which can significantly improve the effectiveness and adaptability of research programs. This approach has broad implications for science policy, funding, and innovation management, benefiting society by enhancing preparedness for crises like pandemics and technological shifts such as AI. The article provides detailed context and examples, making it a substantive and meaningful contribution to advancing scientific practice.
articles.personality_title: Scientists call for future planning to improve research success
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientific research often makes assumptions about the future, like which technologies will develop or what skills will be needed. However, these assumptions are rarely tested, which can cause problems when unexpected events happen, such as a pandemic or rapid advances in artificial intelligence (AI).

**What happened** – Researchers are urging the use of “translational foresight,” a method that helps scientists think ahead by imagining different future scenarios and testing their assumptions. This approach uses tools like the Futures Wheel and scenario analysis to explore possible outcomes and prepare research plans accordingly. These methods have been used in other fields but are not yet common in scientific research.

**Impact** – Using foresight methods can help research programs avoid surprises and adapt better to changes. For example, during COVID-19, scientists had to quickly change their work to develop vaccines and move education online. If foresight had been used before, preparations could have been smoother. This approach can also help manage new challenges like AI by making research and funding decisions more flexible and informed.

**What’s next step** – The article suggests integrating foresight methods into everyday scientific decision-making. Funders and institutions could start testing new technologies and plans before crises happen. They could also set clear signals that tell them when to change direction. This would make science more prepared and responsive to future challenges.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Planning for multiple possible futures can help scientific research stay effective and adaptable in a rapidly changing world.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques appellent à la planification future pour améliorer la réussite de la recherche
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La recherche scientifique repose souvent sur des hypothèses concernant l’avenir, comme les technologies qui se développeront ou les compétences nécessaires. Pourtant, ces hypothèses sont rarement vérifiées, ce qui peut poser problème en cas d’événements imprévus, comme une pandémie ou des avancées rapides de l’intelligence artificielle (IA).

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs encouragent l’utilisation de la « prévoyance translationnelle », une méthode qui aide les scientifiques à anticiper en imaginant différents scénarios futurs et en testant leurs hypothèses. Cette approche utilise des outils comme la Roue des Futurs et l’analyse de scénarios pour explorer les résultats possibles et préparer les plans de recherche. Ces méthodes sont déjà utilisées dans d’autres domaines, mais pas encore courantes en recherche scientifique.

**Impact** – Utiliser ces méthodes de prévoyance peut aider les programmes de recherche à éviter les surprises et à mieux s’adapter aux changements. Par exemple, lors du COVID-19, les scientifiques ont dû rapidement modifier leur travail pour développer des vaccins et passer à l’enseignement en ligne. Avec la prévoyance, ces préparations auraient pu être plus fluides. Cette approche peut aussi aider à gérer de nouveaux défis comme l’IA en rendant les décisions de recherche et de financement plus flexibles et éclairées.

**Prochaine étape** – L’article suggère d’intégrer ces méthodes dans la prise de décision scientifique quotidienne. Les financeurs et institutions pourraient commencer à tester de nouvelles technologies et plans avant les crises. Ils pourraient aussi définir des signaux clairs pour savoir quand changer de direction. Cela rendrait la science plus préparée et réactive face aux défis futurs.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Planifier plusieurs futurs possibles aide la recherche scientifique à rester efficace et adaptable dans un monde en rapide évolution.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos piden planificar el futuro para mejorar el éxito de la investigación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La investigación científica a menudo se basa en suposiciones sobre el futuro, como qué tecnologías se desarrollarán o qué habilidades serán necesarias. Sin embargo, estas suposiciones rara vez se prueban, lo que puede causar problemas cuando ocurren eventos inesperados, como una pandemia o rápidos avances en inteligencia artificial (IA).

**Qué pasó** – Los investigadores están promoviendo el uso de la «previsión traslacional», un método que ayuda a los científicos a pensar en el futuro imaginando diferentes escenarios y probando sus suposiciones. Este enfoque utiliza herramientas como la Rueda de Futuros y el análisis de escenarios para explorar posibles resultados y preparar planes de investigación. Estas técnicas se usan en otros campos, pero aún no son comunes en la investigación científica.

**Impacto** – Usar métodos de previsión puede ayudar a los programas de investigación a evitar sorpresas y adaptarse mejor a los cambios. Por ejemplo, durante la COVID-19, los científicos tuvieron que cambiar rápidamente su trabajo para desarrollar vacunas y pasar a la educación en línea. Si se hubiera usado previsión antes, la preparación habría sido más fácil. Este enfoque también puede ayudar a manejar nuevos desafíos como la IA, haciendo que las decisiones de investigación y financiamiento sean más flexibles y claras.

**Próximo paso** – El artículo sugiere integrar los métodos de previsión en la toma de decisiones científicas diarias. Los financiadores y las instituciones podrían comenzar a probar nuevas tecnologías y planes antes de que ocurran crisis. También podrían establecer señales claras que indiquen cuándo cambiar de rumbo. Esto haría que la ciencia esté mejor preparada y sea más reactiva a futuros desafíos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Planificar varios futuros posibles ayuda a que la investigación científica sea efectiva y adaptable en un mundo que cambia rápidamente.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, intricate network of interconnected translucent gears and light orbs floating above an open blueprint table, symbolizing foresight and planning in scientific research, with subtle silhouettes of futuristic lab equipment and softly glowing horizon lines in the background suggesting evolving technologies and adaptable futures.
